ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for chrysanthemum milk tea; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099) is the closest defensible parent class. Chrysanthemum milk tea with creamer is a speciality beverage not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific code exists for chrysanthemum milk tea in the FoodEx2 hierarchy; this is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for chrysanthemum milk tea as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one cup), equivalent to 250 g, as typically served at Singaporean beverage stalls and cafes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened milk tea beverages — analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chrysanthemum milk tea (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 62 (medium) based on the closest analogous entry: sweetened milk tea with non-dairy creamer, which has a reported GI in the range of 55–70 depending on sugar content. The 'less sugar' formulation (7.22g sugar/100g) places this beverage in the lower-medium GI range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 21.2) / 100 = 13.1, where carbohydrate per 250g serving = 8.48 × 2.5 = 21.2g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (7.22g/100g) exceeds the 5g/100g beverage threshold for hypertension; low sodium (10mg/100g) is favourable but does not offset the sugar-driven cardiovascular risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this beverage due to its added sugar content (7.22g/100g), which independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite its low sodium level of 10mg/100g; an unsweetened chrysanthemum tea without creamer would be a safer alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.22g sugar/100g and 8.48g carbohydrate/100g; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed and raises postprandial blood glucose; dietary fibre at 1.65g/100g is insufficient to meaningfully attenuate glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (7.22g sugar/100g); even the 'less sugar' variant contains sufficient added sugar to trigger rapid postprandial glucose elevation, and only a zero-sugar, unsweetened version would be appropriate for diabetic dietary management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer likely contributes saturated fat (palm oil-derived) and potentially trace trans fat; added sugar at 7.22g/100g further elevates cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation and endothelial dysfunction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage due to the combined risk from added sugar (7.22g/100g) and likely saturated fat from non-dairy creamer; switching to an unsweetened chrysanthemum tea brewed without creamer would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer likely contributes palm oil-derived saturated fat and potentially trace trans fat, both of which are pro-inflammatory; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients are present at clinically relevant levels.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as the non-dairy creamer likely contains pro-inflammatory saturated fat and potentially hydrogenated oils with trans fat; removing toppings does not address the pro-inflammatory fat profile from the creamer base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chrysanthemum milk tea (less sugar, no topping) is a popular Singaporean speciality beverage made by brewing dried chrysanthemum flowers in hot water and blending the floral infusion with non-dairy creamer or milk, then sweetening with a reduced amount of sugar compared to the standard version. The drink has a delicate floral aroma and a mildly sweet, slightly earthy flavour with a creamy texture from the creamer. It is commonly served at local kopitiams, bubble tea shops, and hawker centres across Singapore, and is often consumed as a breakfast or mid-morning drink. Per 100g, it provides 56 kcal, 0g protein (confirmed zero per HPB; this is a beverage with no protein-contributing ingredients at measurable levels), 2.47g fat (primarily from non-dairy creamer), 8.48g carbohydrate, 7.22g sugar, 1.65g dietary fibre, and 10mg sodium. The 'less sugar' designation indicates a reduced but not eliminated sugar content, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension who require zero-sugar beverages. The non-dairy creamer component introduces saturated fat and potentially trace trans fat, raising concerns for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. Chrysanthemum flowers are traditionally associated with anti-inflammatory and cooling properties in Chinese herbal medicine, though the clinical evidence for these effects in a diluted beverage form is limited. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 62 (medium) based on analogous sweetened milk tea data from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycemic load of approximately 13.1 per standard 250ml serving.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the exact food item 'Chrysanthemum milk tea (less sugar, no topping)' with CR ID D08010017, which is an approved primary source for Singaporean foods. All core macronutrient values are taken verbatim from this source with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such.
notes: protein_g is reported as 0.0 per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (D08010017); this is confirmed zero for this beverage formulation — the chrysanthemum flower infusion and non-dairy creamer combination does not contribute measurable protein at the dilution level of this beverage. Confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for chrysanthemum milk tea is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry D08010017; the fat value of 2.47g/100g is attributed primarily to non-dairy creamer but fatty acid fractions are not individually specified in the HPB source. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry D08010017 as of 2026. GI value of 62 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chrysanthemum milk tea (less sugar) exists in any approved GI source as of 2026. The 'less sugar' variant still contains 7.22g sugar/100g and is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage for clinical purposes. Non-dairy creamer composition may vary by vendor; palm oil-derived saturated fat and hydrogenated oil content should be verified against product labels for precise clinical assessment.
